sections.title: Passive Form ~られる / ~(ら)れる
sections.explanation: The passive form expresses that the subject is acted upon. Formation: る-verbs: drop る, add られる: 食べる → 食べられる (to be eaten). う-verbs: change the final -u to -a and add れる: 読む → 読まれる (to be read), 書く → 書かれる (to be written), 話す → 話される (to be spoken). Irregular: する → される (to be done), 来る → 来られる (to be come to). The passive has three main uses in Japanese: 1) Direct passive (like English): この本は多くの人に読まれている (This book is read by many people). 2) Indirect/adversity passive — unique to Japanese — expressing that someone is negatively affected: 雨に降られた (I was rained on — and it was inconvenient). 3) Honorific passive: 先生が話された (The teacher spoke — respectful). The agent is marked with に.
sections.tip: Japanese passive has a unique 'suffering passive' (迷惑の受身) not found in English. 電車で足を踏まれた (I had my foot stepped on in the train) — the subject suffers from someone else's action. Even intransitive verbs can be passive: 彼女に泣かれた (She cried on me — and I was troubled by it).

examples.ja: この曲は世界中で聞かれている。
examples.en: This song is listened to all over the world.
I had my foot stepped on in the train. (adversity passive)
I was praised by the teacher.

examples.ja: 隣の人にたばこを吸われて困った。
examples.en: The person next to me smoked and it bothered me. (adversity)

exercises.prompt: What is the passive form of 書く (kaku, to write)?
exercises.explanation: 書く is an う-verb. Change く to か and add れる: 書く → 書かれる (kakareru, to be written).

exercises.prompt: What is the 'adversity passive' (迷惑の受身)?
options: A passive form used for polite speech
A passive expressing that the subject is negatively affected by someone's action
A passive form only for transitive verbs
exercises.explanation: The adversity passive (迷惑の受身) expresses that the subject suffers or is negatively affected by someone else's action: 雨に降られた (I was rained on and inconvenienced). This usage is unique to Japanese.

exercises.prompt: What is the passive form of する (suru, to do)?
exercises.explanation: する has an irregular passive form: される (sareru, to be done). For example: 実験が行われた is often expressed as 実験がされた.

sections.title: Causative Form ~させる / ~(さ)せる
sections.explanation: The causative form means 'to make/let someone do something.' Formation: る-verbs: drop る, add させる: 食べる → 食べさせる (to make/let eat). う-verbs: change the final -u to -a and add せる: 読む → 読ませる (to make/let read), 書く → 書かせる (to make/let write), 話す → 話させる (to make/let speak). Irregular: する → させる (to make/let do), 来る → 来させる (to make/let come). The causative can mean either 'make' (force) or 'let' (allow), depending on context and particles. With を: 母は子供に野菜を食べさせた (The mother made the child eat vegetables — forced). With を for the person: 先生は学生を帰らせた (The teacher let/made the students go home). Context determines whether it means 'make' or 'let.'
sections.tip: The difference between 'make' and 'let' often depends on context and the verb. 子供を遊ばせる could mean 'let the child play' or 'make the child play.' Adding てあげる clarifies 'let': 遊ばせてあげる (let them play — doing a favor). With てください, it becomes a request: やらせてください (please let me do it).

examples.ja: 母は子供に野菜を食べさせた。
examples.en: The mother made the child eat vegetables.

examples.ja: 先生は学生を帰らせた。
examples.en: The teacher let the students go home.

examples.ja: 部長は新人にコピーを取らせた。
examples.en: The department head had the new employee make copies.

exercises.prompt: What is the causative form of 飲む (nomu, to drink)?
exercises.explanation: 飲む is an う-verb. Change む to ま and add せる: 飲む → 飲ませる (nomaseru, to make/let drink).

exercises.prompt: What does やらせてください mean?
exercises.explanation: やらせてください = causative (やらせる) + てください (please). It means 'please let me do it' — requesting permission to perform the action yourself.

exercises.prompt: What is the causative form of する (suru)?
exercises.explanation: する has an irregular causative form: させる (saseru, to make/let do). For example: 勉強させる (make someone study).

sections.title: Passive-Causative ~させられる — Being Made To
sections.explanation: The passive-causative combines both forms to express 'being made to do something' — the subject is forced by someone else. Formation: take the causative form and make it passive. る-verbs: 食べさせる → 食べさせられる (to be made to eat). う-verbs: 読ませる → 読ませられる (to be made to read). In casual speech, う-verbs often contract: 読ませられる → 読まされる, 飲ませられる → 飲まされる, 行かせられる → 行かされる. This contracted form (～される) is very common in spoken Japanese. Irregular: する → させられる, 来る → 来させられる. The passive-causative always carries a negative nuance — the subject doesn't want to do it but is forced: 毎日残業させられている (I'm being made to work overtime every day).
sections.tip: The contracted form ～される is preferred in speech: 飲まされる instead of 飲ませられる, 歌わされる instead of 歌わせられる. However, る-verbs do NOT contract: 食べさせられる (not 食べさされる). The passive-causative always implies unwillingness — being forced against your will.

examples.ja: 上司に残業させられた。
examples.en: I was made to work overtime by my boss.

examples.ja: 子供の頃、ピアノを習わされた。
examples.en: As a child, I was made to learn piano. (contracted)

examples.ja: パーティーで歌わされた。
examples.en: I was made to sing at the party. (contracted)

examples.ja: 嫌いな野菜を食べさせられた。
examples.en: I was made to eat vegetables I dislike.

exercises.prompt: What nuance does the passive-causative always carry?
options: Being forced to do something against your will
exercises.explanation: The passive-causative always implies that the subject is being forced to do something they don't want to do. It expresses unwillingness and external compulsion.

exercises.prompt: What is the contracted passive-causative of 待つ (matsu, to wait)?
exercises.explanation: Full form: 待たせられる. Contracted: 待たされる (matasareru, to be made to wait). The contraction drops せら and becomes さ: ～ませられる → ～まされる.

exercises.prompt: Can る-verbs use the contracted passive-causative form?
options: No, only う-verbs contract; る-verbs keep the full form
る-verbs use a completely different form
exercises.explanation: Only う-verbs can use the contracted form (～まされる). る-verbs must use the full passive-causative: 食べさせられる (not 食べさされる). This is a key distinction to remember.

sections.title: Comparing Passive, Causative & Passive-Causative
sections.explanation: Let's compare all three forms using 書く (kaku, to write): Passive: 書かれる (kakareru) — 'to be written' / 'something is written by someone.' Causative: 書かせる (kakaseru) — 'to make/let someone write.' Passive-Causative: 書かされる (kakasareru) — 'to be made to write.' The key perspective shifts: Passive puts focus on the receiver of the action. Causative puts focus on the person who causes the action. Passive-Causative puts focus on the person being forced. Example chain: 先生が作文を書いた (The teacher wrote the essay — active). 作文が先生に書かれた (The essay was written by the teacher — passive). 先生は学生に作文を書かせた (The teacher made the students write an essay — causative). 学生は先生に作文を書かされた (The students were made to write an essay by the teacher — passive-causative).
sections.tip: To keep the three forms straight: passive = ~(ら)れる (受身, something happens to you), causative = ~(さ)せる (使役, you make/let someone), passive-causative = ~(さ)せられる (受身使役, someone makes you). The passive-causative is always the longest form and always implies negative feelings.

examples.ja: 先生は学生にレポートを書かせた。(causative)
examples.en: The teacher made the students write a report.

examples.ja: 学生は先生にレポートを書かされた。(passive-causative)
examples.en: The students were made to write a report by the teacher.
I'm being made to run every morning.

exercises.prompt: 母は子供に薬を飲ませた。What form is 飲ませた?
exercises.explanation: 飲ませた is the causative (past tense) of 飲む. The mother (母) caused the child (子供) to drink medicine. The mother is the causer.

exercises.prompt: 学生は毎日漢字を書かされる。What does this mean?
options: Students are able to write kanji.
Students are made to write kanji every day.
exercises.explanation: 書かされる is the passive-causative (contracted) of 書く. The students are being forced to write kanji — they don't have a choice. The negative/forced nuance is key.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence uses the passive form?
options: 先生が学生を立たせた。
先生に宿題をやらされた。
exercises.explanation: 褒められた is the passive of 褒める (to praise): 'I was praised by the teacher.' 立たせた is causative ('made stand'), やらされた is passive-causative ('was made to do'), and 出した is active ('gave out').
